explanation: Gray/black show-through at an implant crown is usually from a metal (titanium/CrCo) abutment shining through thin mucosa or translucent ceramic. Switching to a tooth-colored zirconia abutment reduces the gray line and improves pink-white esthetics.
The incisive papilla is the classic fixed soft-tissue landmark used to orient anterior tooth and implant position in the maxilla (A-P and midline relationship of the central incisors).

Porcelain chipping at try-in is most often from premature heavy occlusal contact (e.g., deep bite/interference) loading the ceramic before cementation; reduction and oxide-layer failures are more fabrication/prep issues.

Long-face pattern with backward-downward mandibular rotation, anterior open bite, proclined incisors, and supererupted posteriors is classically associated with chronic mouth breathing and vertical growth excess.

Primary lateral with multi-surface (incisal + proximal) caries needing esthetics is well managed with a resin-veneered stainless-steel crown when remaining structure is inadequate for a simple composite strip crown alone.

When replacing a missing first molar and the second molar is mesially tilted, a non-rigid (stress-breaking) connector is used so the tilted distal abutment is not locked into a single path that creates harmful torquing forces.
ART (atraumatic restorative treatment) uses hand excavation and adhesive GIC without rotary trauma; it is a conservative restorative/minimally invasive approach, not aggressive surgery.

The simplest repair for a fractured cast clasp arm is often adding a wrought-wire (combination) clasp by soldering/acrylic pick-up, avoiding full framework remake when the rest of the RPD is serviceable.

After loss of the upper anteriors plus planned extraction of UL second premolar, the maxillary RPD needs multiple rests on remaining posterior abutments and several minor connectors/retentive arms; among listed choices, a multi-rest multi-arm design (6 rests / 5 arms) best matches that extensive Class IV–type span.
Hybrid composites combine strength of larger fillers with polishability of smaller particles and remain the most widely used universal restorative composite for anterior and posterior use.

The abutment is the prosthetic component that connects the implant fixture (body) to the crown/bridge. An analog is a lab replica; a coping is a transfer or temporary component, not the final connector.
Addition silicone (PVS) custom trays are typically spaced about 2–3 mm to allow even wash/light-body thickness and accurate elastomeric flow without trapping or distortion.
For oroantral communication, cover oral anaerobes and sinus flora; amoxicillin–clavulanate is a preferred first-line oral regimen (penicillin alone is narrower; metronidazole is adjunctive).

An unintended lip laceration is a medical error/adverse event that caused harm. A near miss is an event that almost happened but did not reach the patient. Disclosure, referral, and apology are appropriate responses, not proof of malpractice by themselves.
Through a PFM crown: use a diamond bur to cut porcelain, then a transmetal/carbide bur to cut the metal coping, then proceed into tooth structure—never reverse that sequence.

When gingival inflammation is a concern, place the new veneer finish line supragingival (or only slightly equigingival after health returns). Deep subgingival margins worsen plaque retention and inflammation despite esthetics.

Polyether is hydrophilic and sensitive to long immersion in some agents; short disinfection with glutaraldehyde (per manufacturer) is classically recommended. Prolonged hypochlorite or alcohol can distort polyether.
A mesially tilted molar abutment for an FPD replacing the first molar is managed with a non-rigid connector on the distal abutment to accommodate divergent paths of insertion and reduce torque.
Pinpoint palatal petechiae/red dots under a maxillary denture are classic Newton-type denture stomatitis (Candida). Treat with antifungal therapy plus denture hygiene and night removal.
Alginate is the common irreversible hydrocolloid used for study casts and many RPD preliminary impressions. Agar is reversible hydrocolloid; addition silicone is an elastomer, not hydrocolloid.

Cervical abrasion/caries with lost restoration and dentin sensitivity is treated first by restoring the defect (composite or GIC) to seal dentin; free gingival graft is reserved for mucogingival defects, not the primary fix for sensitivity.
Moisture contamination of alginate powder produces incomplete wetting and a grainy, inhomogeneous mix that yields a poor surface; keep powder sealed and dry.
Lithium disilicate needs adhesive resin cementation for support. Non-adhesive zinc phosphate does not bond/support the ceramic, so thin ceramic can crack under function within weeks.
Immediate allergic reaction to latex rubber dam (urticaria, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis) is Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity; delayed contact dermatitis is Type IV.
Bond strength is lowest in deep dentin (higher tubule density, more moisture, less intertubular dentin) compared with enamel or superficial dentin—critical in large MOD composites.
Multiple cervical/root caries benefit from resin-modified GIC: chemical adhesion to dentin, fluoride release, and moisture tolerance superior to pure composite at root surfaces.
If border-molded final impression has deficient areas, selectively correct those regions (add material in deficient zones and re-mold) rather than heat-soaking or yanking the tray incorrectly.
With peri-implant soft-tissue inflammation (BOP, rolled gingiva) and no radiographic excess cement, check prosthetic stability—loose abutment screw is a common reversible cause of local inflammation.
Posterior crown esthetics and prep design are strongly influenced by the functional (supporting) cusp of the premolar—bevel/reduction and contour there affect shade display and occlusal form.

Sore/discomfort after CD delivery is commonly from overextended or thick flanges impinging on movable mucosa. Pure post-dam underextension causes loss of seal more than pain; overextended PPS causes sore throat.

Unilateral long-term chewing with loss of chewing efficiency on that side after years usually reflects worn flat occlusal anatomy on the preferred side—restore cuspal form (or remake teeth) rather than only reline if base fit/VDO are acceptable.

For routine complete dentures, a semi-adjustable articulator set near average condylar guidance (~20–30°, often 25°) is standard; monoplane/nonanatomic teeth are reserved for poor ridges or special cases.
All upper incisors missing is a single anterior edentulous area crossing the midline (Kennedy Class IV). Adding a separate second-premolar extraction space is a modification: Class IV mod 1.
Microfill composites use colloidal silica fillers on the order of ~0.01–0.04 µm, giving high polish but lower strength than hybrids.
Small palatal papules under a denture worn 24 hours/day are inflammatory papillary hyperplasia (Candida-related), not epulis fissuratum (flange fold) or smoker’s palate alone.

A dark line after cementing a translucent ceramic often reflects show-through of a non-opaque or discolored cement/interface; switch to an appropriate opaque-capable adhesive resin cement system and correct substrate masking.

Isolated second premolars standing alone with bilateral distal-extension Kennedy II mod 2 are poor lone abutments (high leverage). Extraction of hopeless isolated premolars and a simpler design is often preferred over ring-clasping weak teeth.

The mandibular anterior (interforaminal) region has the most favorable anatomy with least major neurovascular risk for implants. Posterior mandible risks IAN; posterior maxilla has sinus; anterior maxilla has nasopalatine canal.
Ignoring anterior guidance when replacing anteriors allows posterior interferences in excursion, which can produce TMJ/muscle strain and occlusal trauma—not merely a shade/misfit issue.

Active disease (heavy calculus, multiple caries) must be controlled with scaling/root planing and caries control before or concurrent with orthodontics; definitive crowns come after disease control and preferably after ortho stabilization.
Crown-down creates early coronal flare so files engage apically with less torsional stress and better irrigant access, unlike classic step-back that enlarges apex first.

Setting mandibular posterior teeth steeply lingual to the ridge crowds the tongue and directs unfavorable forces; chronic anterior loading contributes to residual ridge resorption in the anterior mandible.

If a multi-unit bridge rocks at try-in because one retainer is ill-fitting, evaluate seating with disclosing media, section the framework at the bad joint, and solder/laser-weld after accurate reassembly—do not force-cement.

In pregnancy, clindamycin is an accepted alternative antibiotic when beta-lactams cannot be used. Tetracyclines stain teeth/bones; many macrolides are Category C; fluconazole is an antifungal, not a routine antibacterial.
Multiple untreated abscesses/caries and neglected medical/dental care in a child constitute dental neglect (a form of child neglect), not primarily sexual or physical abuse.

A 3-year-old CD that lost retention with intact teeth, acceptable occlusion/VDO, and serviceable base is managed by reline (tissue conditioner then hard reline) to re-adapt the intaglio—not full remake first-line.
For immediate denture extractions in a quadrant/arch, remove posteriors first (allow healing/ridge molding) and leave anteriors until delivery day for esthetics and VDO reference.
With healthy tissues and only smile dissatisfaction, start conservatively by reshaping/restoring anterior tooth form (enameloplasty/composite) before committing to full veneers.
Bleeding on probing around an implant with radiographic excess cement is cement-induced peri-implant mucositis/peri-implantitis risk—remove residual cement and reassess soft tissue.
Among restorative preps, full-coverage crowns remove the most tooth structure and generate the greatest pulp risk/necrosis rate compared with inlays/onlays.
Bleeding from the chamber while removing a temporary filling usually means residual vital pulp tissue (incomplete prior debridement) rather than enamel at the CEJ.
After pulpotomy/pulpectomy in primary teeth, full-coverage SSC best prevents fracture and microleakage and has the fewest long-term complications versus multi-surface composite/GIC alone.

Zinc polycarboxylate chemically chelates to tooth calcium (true chemical adhesion). Zinc phosphate is micromechanical only; conventional resin cements rely mainly on micromechanical interlocking plus adhesive systems.

After surgical crown lengthening, wait for soft-tissue and biologic-width stability before final impression—commonly about 6–8 weeks for posterior sites (longer, ~3 months, if anterior esthetics are critical).

Midline fracture of an old maxillary complete denture is classically related to flexure of an ill-fitting base under function (also midline notch/thinness and buccalized teeth); poorly fitting bases rock and stress the midline.
Final impression after crown lengthening should wait until gingival margins stabilize—typically 6–8 weeks in general practice messaging (extend toward 3 months for highly esthetic anterior cases).
Camouflage/Class III mechanics commonly retrocline (lingually tip) the lower incisors; that is a frequent dental side effect of Class III elastics/compensation.
In a young child with multiple proximal primary caries, stainless-steel crowns outperform multi-surface intracoronal restorations for durability and seal when caries is extensive.
If the minor connector/guiding plate does not contact the guide plane properly with a circumferential clasp assembly, the abutment receives improper lateral forces during insertion and function.

Heavy anterior contact with no posterior contact means the occlusion must be rebuilt/checked to restore simultaneous posterior support; reassess centric stops after corrective posterior restoration rather than ignoring the open posterior bite.
Using a company sample implant for a patient without transparent disclosure of industry relationships violates conflict-of-interest ethics more than confidentiality/privacy alone.

In cirrhosis, avoid NSAIDs (bleeding, renal injury). Acetaminophen at reduced total daily dose is generally preferred systemic analgesic; many other listed agents also need caution but NSAIDs are particularly unsafe.
Honestly explaining that a sound amalgam does not need replacement and declining unnecessary treatment demonstrates veracity (truthfulness), not justice or autonomy alone.
A complete denture fractured through the palate after ~9 years of service is best managed by remake (or laboratory repair then remake if fit is poor); reline alone does not fix a broken base.
Excessive cast tilt on the surveyor creates a false path of insertion and unfavorable undercuts, making the RPD hard to seat and remove and stressing abutments.
Missing 21 and 13 are two separate tooth-bounded spaces that do not form a single anterior edentulous area crossing the midline as one Class IV span—Kennedy Class III modification 1.
Maintain at least ~3 mm between adjacent implant platforms to preserve crestal bone and inter-implant papilla (Tarnow).
Access through all-ceramic requires diamond burs to cut ceramic efficiently; carbide/transmetal are for metal after porcelain is penetrated on PFMs.
Multi-surface caries in primary teeth are best restored with stainless-steel crowns for longevity and full coverage, superior to multi-surface amalgam/composite in high-caries children.
For Class V isolation on a canine, the No. 212 (cervical) clamp is designed to retract gingiva and stabilize dam for facial cervical lesions.
Endodontic access through a ceramic crown starts with a diamond bur to penetrate porcelain/ceramic before switching instruments as needed for metal or dentin.
Deepening the pulpal floor to place it on sound dentin and create a flat seat is part of resistance form (resisting fracture/displacement under load), not outline or retention grooves alone.
An angulated implant with a closed-tray coping that does not draw requires an open-tray (pick-up) impression technique so the coping can be removed with the impression accurately.

If margins sealed at try-in but a large gap appears after cementation, the crown likely seated incompletely on cement—remove, clean preparation and intaglio thoroughly, and recement carefully; remake if margin is still open.
Rocking of a multi-unit bridge at try-in indicates framework misfit—section the bridge, seat individual retainers, and solder/weld, or remake if distortion is severe.

Replacing failed Class I/II amalgams with sensitivity today is typically done with bonded composite (after diagnosis of cause); gold is not first-line for sensitivity complaints and GIC is less ideal for large occlusal load-bearing Class II.
Overtrituration of amalgam produces a hot, shiny, sticky mix that sets faster and has deteriorated mechanical properties (related to mercury/matrix changes)—not easier carving of an ideal mix.
Acute dental abscess with severe pain and swelling needs incision and drainage plus antibiotics when indicated; hypothyroidism is a background risk but does not replace source control with I&D.
In complete denture construction, relating the maxilla to the hinge axis/TMJ (facebow transfer) is recorded so casts mount correctly before refining centric relation and occlusion.
Peri-implant tissues lack a periodontal ligament vascular plexus; blood supply around implants is less robust than around natural teeth with PDL.
Final impressions after crown lengthening should wait for tissue maturation—commonly 6–8 weeks (longer if anterior esthetic gingival levels must fully stabilize).

The periodontist should give an independent evidence-based plan and explain findings to the patient without disparaging the referring GP; reporting “malpractice” or ignoring communication is inappropriate.

Dislodgement on upper lip pull usually means inadequate labial frenum relief or overextended labial flange interfering with frenal movement—not missing occlusal rests (CD has none) or PPS (posterior seal).
If only a clasp is broken and the framework fits, the simplest repair is often a wrought-wire replacement clasp rather than full remake.
Among the listed RPD parts, the denture base transmits occlusal load to residual ridge soft tissue/bone (especially distal extensions). Rests (not listed) transmit load to teeth; clasps retain.
A single crown in a patient with good existing occlusion is fabricated on a semi-adjustable articulator—sufficient for routine fixed prosthodontics without full-adjustable complexity.
Initial imaging for suspected vertical root fracture is a well-angulated periapical radiograph; CBCT is next if 2D is inconclusive. Bitewing is not the primary VRF view.
Cast posts that are too wide (excessive dentin removal) and/or short predispose to vertical root fracture; 9 mm pockets and VRF history fit this mechanical risk pattern.

Recent post-crown with pain and metallic taste but no mobility/radiolucency often reflects occlusal interference (eccentric prematurity) or early galvanic symptoms—adjust occlusion first; VRF usually shows later signs.
Implant diameters ~3.5–4.1 mm are regular/standard platform; narrow is typically ≤3.0–3.3 and wide ≥4.5–5.0 mm depending on system.
Only 4 mm interarch space is limited for a cement-retained stack; a screw-retained restoration uses less restorative height and is preferred in reduced vertical space.
For a single implant, complete papilla fill is predictable when the contact point to crestal bone distance is ≤5 mm (Tarnow); greater distances risk black triangles.
D4 soft bone is a major risk factor for poor primary stability and long-term implant complications. D2 is generally most favorable; D1 can be dense/avascular but is not the classic “worst prognosis.”
D2 bone (thick cortical with dense trabeculae) offers the best combination of stability and vascularity for implants among Lekholm–Zarb types.
Cooperative pediatric patient with multiple caries and plaque often needs durable full-coverage SSCs on extensively involved primary molars rather than multi-surface composites alone.
Cementum is deposited continuously throughout life; increased apical radiopacity around roots in older adults often reflects cumulative cementum deposition (and related apical changes).
In a bruxer with fractured amalgam, a cast (metal or indirect) restoration better resists heavy occlusal load than large direct composite or ceramic inlay alone.
Pain between new amalgam and opposing/adjacent gold is classic galvanic shock—manage by replacing/isolating the amalgam or separating metals once pulpitis is excluded.
Arrested root caries with hard dark surfaces is managed with topical fluoride and risk control, not automatic restoration; restore only if cavitated/active or sensitive.
With remaining dentin thickness about 0.5 mm under a Class I, place a protective RMGI liner/base before the final restoration to reduce thermal/chemical insult.
With ~2 mm remaining dentin under amalgam, a base is generally unnecessary; sealing with varnish/adhesive as indicated is enough—avoid over-bulking bases that weaken residual tooth.

For metal partial veneer (3/4) occlusal reduction depth-orientation grooves, functional cusps need more reduction than nonfunctional; among options, the intermediate functional/nonfunctional groove depths (~0.8–1.3 mm range) best match conservative metal partial coverage before finishing to final reduction.
Well-demarcated erythema and burning under a maxillary denture base is denture stomatitis (usually Candida), common in smokers and continuous wearers—not primary chemical burn.
High-speed cutting without coolant risks pulp overheating and necrosis—stop, restore water spray, then continue; do not keep cutting dry under load.
D4 bone quality is the greatest long-term risk factor for implant failure among D1–D4 classifications due to poor density and stability.
Rigidly connecting a natural tooth to an implant in an FPD fails because the tooth moves in the PDL while the implant is ankylosed—differential mobility leads to cement failure/debonding.
Setting lower denture teeth on a steep slope from ridge up to the retromolar pad directs unfavorable forces and can create problems with tongue space and anterior mandibular ridge loading.

Morning jaw stiffness/pain plus systemic inflammatory joint disease history (knee replacement) points toward rheumatoid arthritis involvement of TMJ more than pure osteoarthritis (which worsens with use later in the day).
The articular disc most commonly displaces anteriorly and medially relative to the condyle in internal derangement.

PVS is relatively stable and compatible with many chemical disinfectants after rinse; impressions still must be disinfected—never send only water-rinsed to the lab. Alginate needs prompt pour; PVS is more forgiving but not “no disinfect.”
Painless reciprocal clicking with normal opening is anterior disc displacement with reduction. Without reduction, opening is limited; OA has pain/crepitus.
Facial soft-tissue recession at implants is most often from overly buccal implant position (placement) relative to the bony envelope; angulation and excess cement are secondary contributors.
With lingual vestibule depth >~7–8 mm, a lingual bar major connector is preferred. Lingual plate is used when depth is insufficient or when anterior teeth need stabilization.
Without the image, the bank description of functional-cusp interference matches a working-side/functional cusp contact prematurity pattern commonly tested in fixed prosthodontics occlusion diagrams.
A reciprocal arm must brace the tooth before or as the retentive tip flexes into the undercut. If reciprocity occurs after retention engages, the abutment is torqued—improper force.
Maxillary first molars often have the poorest periodontal prognosis among listed teeth because of trifurcation anatomy and complex root morphology when severe periodontitis is present.

Asymptomatic midline/base-of-tongue mass in a child may be lingual thyroid—do not excise first. Image (MRI/thyroid scan) to confirm ectopic thyroid and that cervical thyroid exists before any removal.

Middle-aged African-descent women with periapical radiopaque/mixed lesions of vital mandibular teeth classically have cemento-osseous dysplasia (florid/periapical)—not condensing osteitis of nonvital teeth.

When a specialist disagrees with a referred crown plan, communicate professionally with the referring clinic/report findings and discuss alternatives—avoid simply telling the patient the other dentist was “wrong” in a disparaging way.
Keep at least 2 mm safety zone between the implant and the inferior alveolar canal to reduce nerve injury risk.

Recommended implant-to-adjacent-tooth distance is about 1.5 mm (often taught as ≥1.5–2 mm). Among given choices, 0.5 mm is the only nonzero sub-5 mm option and is the least incorrect distance listed (5 mm is not the minimum).
Inverting the rubber dam edge into the sulcus creates a seal that prevents saliva seepage into the operating field.
G.V. Black Class III is proximal caries of anterior teeth that does not involve the incisal angle.
Class VI lesions involve incisal edges of anteriors or cusp tips of posteriors (wear/attrition sites).
Retention form resists dislodgement of the restoration from tensile/pulling forces along the path of withdrawal.
Gamma-2 (Sn-Hg) in low-copper amalgam corrodes and weakens the restoration; high-copper alloys eliminate most gamma-2.
Glass ionomer cements release fluoride and can recharge, a property used in caries-risk restorations and liners.
Bases under deep amalgam historically provided thermal insulation and a mechanical platform for condensation.
IRM (reinforced ZOE) is used as an intermediate/temporary restorative material, not a permanent esthetic restoration.
Abrasion is mechanical wear from foreign objects (e.g., aggressive toothbrushing, pipes), distinct from erosion or attrition.
Bitewings best detect proximal caries and crestal bone levels with minimal overlap and good enamel resolution.
Carious demineralization appears radiolucent (darker) relative to sound enamel/dentin on radiographs.
Radiopaque restoratives allow detection of overhangs, voids, and secondary caries adjacent to restorations on x-ray.
Primary teeth have thinner enamel, larger pulps, and broader contacts—prep design and depth differ from permanent teeth.
Repair of old composite is acceptable when the defect is localized, remaining restoration and tooth are sound, and isolation is possible.
Bulk-fill composites are formulated for deeper light-cure and thicker increments than traditional 2 mm layering composites.
Silane couples resin to silica-based ceramics and glass fillers by creating a chemical bridge after etching.
Early childhood (nursing bottle) caries classically devastates maxillary primary incisors while mandibular incisors are often spared by the tongue and saliva.
Celluloid strip crowns are used for esthetic full-coverage composite restorations of primary anterior teeth.
Tunnel preparations access proximal caries while attempting to preserve the marginal ridge; they are technique-sensitive.
Pooled thick adhesive films cure incompletely, weaken the interface, and can cause postoperative sensitivity.
Preheating composite lowers viscosity (better adaptation) and may improve degree of conversion depending on protocol.
Giomers use PRG (pre-reacted glass ionomer) filler technology marketed for fluoride release/recharge with composite handling.
Chairside hardness of remaining dentin is judged mainly by tactile resistance with an excavator plus visual cues.
Lactobacilli are associated with progression of established carious lesions in high-sugar, low-pH environments.
Gold onlays offer excellent wear compatibility and longevity and can be more conservative than full crowns when indicated.
Die spacer creates controlled cement space so castings/crowns seat fully without binding on axial walls.
Provisionals protect pulp, maintain tooth position and occlusion, and support gingival health until the definitive restoration.
Fiber posts are generally easier to retrieve (drilling along fiber) than well-adapted cast metal posts if endodontic retreatment is needed.
Lithium disilicate cementation typically uses HF etch, silane, and adhesive resin cement per manufacturer protocol.
Horizontal/lateral forces are most destructive to RPD abutments; vertical forces along the long axis are better tolerated.
Lingual bar needs adequate functional depth from free gingival margin to floor of mouth (often ≥7–8 mm) plus clearance for the bar.
The buccal shelf is a primary stress-bearing area for mandibular complete dentures because of dense cortical bone and favorable mucosa.
The vibrating line helps locate the posterior limit of the maxillary denture and the posterior palatal seal area.
Border molding records functional depth and width of vestibules so the flange can achieve peripheral seal without overextension.
Balanced occlusion aims for multi-tooth contacts in eccentric excursions to stabilize complete denture bases against tipping.
Gamma-2 in low-copper amalgam causes corrosion and weakness; high-copper amalgams minimize gamma-2.
Creep is time-dependent plastic deformation under load; high-copper amalgams exhibit less creep than low-copper alloys.
Microfilled composites polish well but are weak under load and fracture; replace with a hybrid/nanohybrid that balances strength and polish for functional restorations.
When one abutment is severely tilted, a fixed-movable bridge with a non-rigid connector allows a workable path of insertion without over-reducing teeth.
GIC Type I is the luting type for cementing crowns and bridges; Type II is restorative; Type III is liner/base.
For maximum esthetic hiding of the finish line, margins are placed subgingival (after tissue health), accepting more periodontal risk than supragingival placement.
Bulbous crowns, cervical constriction, and abnormal dentin are classic for dentinogenesis imperfecta (not amelogenesis imperfecta, which is enamel).
With 14 mm mesiodistal space, two ~4 mm implants plus ~3 mm inter-implant and ~1.5 mm tooth-implant gaps on each side fit spacing rules better than one large or three mini implants.
Crown fracture involving enamel, dentin, and pulp is a complicated crown fracture; uncomplicated excludes pulp exposure.
Polyvinyl siloxane (addition silicone) offers excellent dimensional accuracy and is the workhorse elastomer for fixed prosthodontic impressions.
Growing skeletal Class III with maxillary deficiency is treated with facemask/reverse-pull protraction therapy (often with expansion), not Class II headgear.
Centric relation records a reproducible maxillomandibular relationship independent of tooth contact for diagnosis and prosthesis construction.
In one-stage implant surgery, a healing abutment (transmucosal) is placed at implant placement so a second-stage uncovering is not required.
Skeletal Class II from maxillary protrusion is managed with distalizing/restraining headgear; reverse-pull is for Class III deficiency.
Ethics require recommending the most conservative effective option; explain alternatives and obtain informed consent rather than automatically doing requested veneers.
Kennedy Class I is bilateral distal-extension edentulous areas posterior to remaining natural teeth.
If dam wrinkles between teeth, holes were punched too far apart; holes too close tear or stretch excessively.
High occlusion after new composite is managed by immediate occlusal adjustment—first action before remakes or meds.
An interference occurring during forward (protrusive) movement is a protrusive interference.
